incident_summary:
Male patient in the 60-69 age range suffered a witnessed out-of-hospital cardiac arrest at a private residence. Bystander CPR was initiated by the patient's spouse approximately 90 seconds before EMS arrival. On arrival the patient was pulseless and apneic with an initial rhythm of ventricular fibrillation.

Medic 51 (P-001 primary, P-002 secondary) performed ACLS including continuous compressions, two defibrillations at 200J biphasic, IV access, three rounds of epinephrine 1mg IV, and placement of an i-gel supraglottic airway. Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) was achieved at approximately eight minutes from incident start, with a post-ROSC rhythm of normal sinus at 92 bpm.

Patient was packaged and transported to the regional cardiac receiving center with continuous monitoring. Total scene time was approximately 11 minutes. This review identified one critical timing discrepancy and two concerns warranting QA follow-up; ACLS adherence was acceptable but with documented deviations in epinephrine interval and airway timing.

timeline.canonical_description: EMS arrives on scene; patient pulseless, apneic, bystander CPR in progress.

source_events.description: Arrived on scene at private residence; patient supine on living room floor, bystander CPR in progress by spouse.
source_events.raw_evidence: PCR narrative: 'Unit 14 arrived on scene at 14:02. Pt found supine on living room floor with spouse performing chest compressions. Pt was pulseless and apneic on assessment.'

source_events.description: Body-cam shows medic entering residence and approaching patient; spouse providing compressions.
source_events.raw_evidence: Video timestamp 00:02:03 — front door opens, two medics enter, kit drops at 00:02:11, spouse audibly counting compressions.
Radio traffic 14:01:58 — Medic 14: 'Dispatch, Unit 14 on scene, going in.'

timeline.canonical_description: First defibrillation at 200J biphasic for ventricular fibrillation.

source_events.description: First shock delivered at 200J biphasic; rhythm pre-shock ventricular fibrillation.
source_events.raw_evidence: PCR interventions: 'Defib #1 at 14:04 — 200J biphasic, V-fib. CPR resumed immediately post-shock.'

source_events.description: Monitor screen visible in body-cam; shock delivered, observable patient muscle contraction.
source_events.raw_evidence: Video 00:04:14 — 'Clear!' verbalized; 00:04:18 — shock button pressed, patient torso jerks, monitor shows post-shock asystole transitioning to fine V-fib.

timeline.canonical_description: Return of spontaneous circulation; carotid pulse palpable, monitor shows narrow-complex rhythm at 92 bpm.

source_events.description: ROSC achieved. Post-ROSC rhythm: NSR at 92 bpm. Carotid pulse strong and regular.
source_events.raw_evidence: PCR narrative: 'ROSC at 14:08. Carotid palpable, monitor NSR @ 92, SpO2 84% on BVM ventilation.'

source_events.description: Body-cam captures medic verbalizing 'I have a pulse' and pulse-check on carotid.
source_events.raw_evidence: Video 00:08:02 — Medic 1: 'Hold compressions — I've got a pulse, strong carotid.' Monitor visible showing organized rhythm.

clinical_assessment.benchmark: Arrival to patient contact within 60 seconds of entering scene
clinical_assessment.notes: Body-cam shows kit drop and patient contact within ~30 seconds of front-door entry at 02:03.

clinical_assessment.benchmark: Rhythm identified within 30 seconds of patient contact
clinical_assessment.notes: Pads applied and V-fib confirmed by 02:30; first shock at 04:00 follows established assessment.

clinical_assessment.benchmark: EMS chest compressions started within 10 seconds of confirming arrest
clinical_assessment.notes: Bystander CPR was already in progress; EMS assumed compressions within ~25s of contact (within target).

clinical_assessment.benchmark: Peri-shock pause less than 10 seconds for each defibrillation
clinical_assessment.notes: Body-cam confirms <10s hands-off across both shocks; pads pre-positioned, charge-during-CPR technique used.

clinical_assessment.benchmark: Advanced airway placed within 5 minutes of arrest when feasible
clinical_assessment.notes: i-gel placed at 06:30 from incident start (~04:30 post-arrival), 90s past the 5-minute soft target. BVM ventilation effective during the delay.

clinical_assessment.benchmark: IV/IO established within first 5 minutes of EMS arrival
clinical_assessment.notes: Successful 18g antecubital line documented; an unsuccessful EJ attempt visible on body-cam at 03:42 is not documented in the PCR.

clinical_assessment.benchmark: Epinephrine 1mg IV every 3-5 minutes during cardiac arrest
clinical_assessment.notes: Interval between dose 2 and dose 3 was 5 min 50 s per body-cam — exceeds the 3-5 minute window. Dose 1-to-2 interval was within range.

clinical_assessment.benchmark: VF/pulseless VT shocked within 2 minutes of identification
clinical_assessment.notes: Both shocks delivered at 200J biphasic with anterior-lateral pad placement immediately following rhythm identification.

clinical_assessment.benchmark: Continuous ECG monitoring with EtCO2 if advanced airway in place
clinical_assessment.notes: Continuous ECG visible on monitor through transport; EtCO2 waveform documented at 28 mmHg post-airway placement.

clinical_assessment.benchmark: Structured handoff to receiving hospital with full ACLS timeline
clinical_assessment.notes: PCR references 'verbal report given' but does not record the structured-handoff fields (rhythm history, drug timing, ROSC time) per agency policy.

issues: First epinephrine dose timestamp in PCR is 30s earlier than body-cam evidence; downstream dose intervals computed from the incorrect anchor.
Unsuccessful external jugular IV attempt at 03:42 visible on body-cam is not documented in the PCR procedures section.
Rhythm check at 06:00 charted in PCR is not corroborated by body-cam or dispatch audio and appears to be a reconstruction.

findings.title: First epinephrine dose documented 30 seconds earlier than video evidence
findings.explanation: PCR records first epinephrine 1mg IV administered at 04:05 from incident start. Body-cam video shows the syringe pushed at 04:35 with verbal callout 'epi in.' This 30-second timing discrepancy is significant because subsequent epinephrine doses are charted relative to the (incorrect) first dose, propagating the error through the ACLS interval reconstruction.
findings.pcr_excerpt: Medications: 'Epinephrine 1mg IV push at 14:04:05 — administered by Medic 2 via 18g antecubital line.'
findings.suggested_review_action: Reconcile medication timestamps with body-cam clock; correct charted times in PCR before submission to QA. Re-check intervals to confirm subsequent doses still met 3-5 min spacing requirement.

findings.title: Second IV access attempt visible on video but absent from PCR
findings.explanation: Body-cam captures a 16g external jugular IV attempt by Medic 1 at 03:42 from incident start, following a failed antecubital attempt. Only the successful antecubital line is documented in the PCR. While the EJ attempt was unsuccessful, the procedure should be charted including indication and outcome per agency policy.
findings.pcr_excerpt: Procedures: '18g IV antecubital, first attempt successful, no extravasation noted.' (No mention of EJ attempt.)
findings.suggested_review_action: Add EJ access attempt to PCR procedures section; include indication (initial AC attempt failed), time, and outcome. Crew education on documenting unsuccessful procedures.

findings.title: PCR documents a rhythm check at 06:00 that is not corroborated by video or audio
findings.explanation: PCR lists a rhythm check at 06:00 from incident start showing 'persistent V-fib.' Body-cam shows continuous compressions during this window with no pause for rhythm assessment, and dispatch audio contains no rhythm callout in this period. The next observed rhythm check on video occurs at 06:18. The 06:00 entry is likely a charting estimate rather than an actual interruption of CPR.
findings.pcr_excerpt: Rhythm checks: '14:06 — V-fib, continued CPR.' Listed between epinephrine entries.
findings.suggested_review_action: Confirm actual rhythm-check time with crew; if reconstructed/estimated, annotate as such in PCR and remove from billable interventions list.

findings.title: Advanced airway placement deferred until 06:30, slightly later than recommended target
findings.explanation: Agency ACLS protocol recommends advanced airway placement within 5 minutes of arrest recognition when feasible. The i-gel was placed at 06:30 from incident start (~04:30 from EMS arrival). Crew documentation indicates BVM ventilation was effective during the delay and the priority was uninterrupted compressions, which is a defensible clinical decision. Flagged for awareness only.
findings.pcr_excerpt: Airway: 'i-gel size 4 placed at 14:06:30 — first attempt successful, ETCO2 waveform confirmed at 28 mmHg.'
findings.suggested_review_action: No action required. Suggest noting BVM-vs-advanced-airway decision rationale in narrative for future QA clarity.

protocol_step.description: Initiate or confirm high-quality CPR within 60 seconds of patient contact for confirmed cardiac arrest.

protocol_checks.explanation: Bystander CPR was already in progress on EMS arrival. EMS assumed compressions within 25 seconds of patient contact (video confirms transition at 02:28 from incident start). Within target.

protocol_step.description: Administer epinephrine 1mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes during cardiac arrest until ROSC or termination.

protocol_checks.explanation: Three epinephrine doses given. Interval between dose 2 and dose 3 was 5 minutes 50 seconds per body-cam timestamps, exceeding the 3-5 minute window. Dose 1-to-2 interval (4:00) was within range.

protocol_step.description: Perform rhythm check every 2 minutes during ACLS resuscitation.

protocol_checks.explanation: Rhythm checks observed at 02:30, 04:14, 06:18, and 08:00 from incident start. All intervals within ±15 seconds of the 2-minute target. Note: PCR-documented 06:00 check appears to be a charting estimate (see finding fnd_03).

protocol_step.description: Defibrillate at 200J biphasic immediately upon identification of ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia.

protocol_checks.explanation: Two shocks delivered for V-fib at 200J biphasic. Both followed identification of shockable rhythm with minimal hands-off time (<10 seconds). Pads positioned anterior-lateral per protocol.

protocol_step.description: Establish advanced airway (supraglottic or endotracheal) within 5 minutes of arrest when feasible without compromising compressions.

protocol_checks.explanation: i-gel supraglottic placed at 06:30 from incident start (04:30 post-arrival), 90 seconds beyond the 5-minute soft target. BVM ventilation was effective during the delay; deviation is minor and clinically defensible (see finding fnd_04).

recommendations.description: Crew P-001 and P-002 should chart medication timestamps directly from the monitor or body-cam clock rather than reconstructing them post-event. Discrepant epinephrine timing in this case propagated through subsequent dose intervals.
Document all attempted procedures including unsuccessful ones (e.g. the EJ IV attempt at 03:42). Include indication, technique, and outcome per agency policy.
Add a structured-handoff field set to the standard PCR template (rhythm history, drug timing, ROSC time) to reduce reliance on free-text narrative for ACLS handoff information.

Schedule a peer review session with Medic 51 crew to discuss the medication-timing discrepancy and BVM-vs-advanced-airway decision rationale. Non-punitive, learning-oriented per Just Culture principles.

determination_rationale: Determination is performance_concern because the case includes one critical timing-discrepancy finding (fnd_01_epi_timing) that propagates through downstream medication intervals, plus two NOT_MET clinical assessment items (advanced airway timing and epinephrine interval). ROSC was achieved and patient outcome was favorable, but the documentation inaccuracy and protocol deviations warrant crew-level coaching and an updated PCR before submission.
